channel.title: Free Open Source IGP on FOSS IGP
channel.description: Recent content in Free Open Source IGP on FOSS IGP

item.title: Griz BH1 4 months
item.description:
Here’s Griz’s attempt at the BH trial on the same day as Argo’s.

Fun fact she’s Argo’s little sister.

item.title: Fetch Obedience
item.description: I’m still working all the stuff from BH trial test here. Just I’m mixing it up as a fun little game of fetch with Griz. Good to see how she’s doing

item.title: Weapon_training_first_thoughts
item.description: I was showing this to some of my military buddies, and he brought up a pretty good point about it. The likelyhood of needing Griz to attack an intruder when I have a weapon is pretty low. Most likely the situation wouldn’t call for a weapon and a dog, and if it did, there’s going to be legal issues where we look at prison and a dead dog. So the idea of an intruder drill needs to be trained with this in mind. We need to think situations through and ensure that we respond in a defensive manner. This means we don’t send the dog on someone running away. We probably don’t want to send the dog initially. I think this shows how good IGP is, because what we want is a scary dog while we “talk” someone off the property.

item.title: Diet
item.description: You can feed your dog kibble, alot of people do and have dogs that live long full lives. I had 1 amazing dog who couldn’t digest kibble which forced us down a long road of raw food. We’ve done it alot of ways. At times we saved money by getting stuff from farmers to include livers, hearts, kidnies and all that. That turned out to be gross for me and gross for the dogs. Yeah that’s what I said, dogs don’t like the way organs taste. I’ve checked with like 5 dogs and 5 out of 5 didn’t like the organs. they would eat it if i forced them by not feeding them, but they would pass if they weren’t starving. I then moved to premade raw grinds for a while and that was ok. But if you watch documentaries, you’ll notice that even the wolves eat berries and grass and non meat items from time to time, and we use fruit and veggies, and supplements in kibble already so we ended up looking for non-meat options to get the micro-nutrients. You can make your own and save money but i lack time, so I use dr. harvey’s. The instructions say to mix it with hot water, meat, oil and serve. But i find that if you i do that, then denali won’t eat it. so instead of hot water, i mix it with an egg, atleast for dinner when denali is eating. In the morning i just mix it with hot water and some meat for griz. She’ll eat anything.

item.title: Tracking 17weeks
item.description: New lines. Making some mistakes on this tracking line with the size of the food. Other than that she’s getting better

item.title: Hurddle 1
item.description: I got the one hurrdle, Griz can jump this one now without assistance. We work on down/stays, Sit/stays, retrieve, and control.

item.title: Dan Argo BH1
item.description:
reply at the bottom

Dan is a new friend of mine we met through our breeder. Argo is actually Griz’s big brother from the litter before. He’s sitting at 11 months compared to Griz’s 4 months. I’m super excited to have someone else to work with. Argo is got a gnarly bite on him. His baseline seems to be to clamp and pull. And the POWER!! . Man he’s strong.

item.title: IGP RULES
item.description:
ruleset

Basic Requirements for dog behavior

  • Confidence
  • Free, happy, motivated to work
  • Attentive to the handler
  • Harmony of the team whatever that means
  • Correctness
  • Acceptance of commands
  • Basic assesment of execution of trial exercises
  • Response to verbal

Verbal corrections

  • 1 additional command is 1.5 deduction
  • 2nd additional commands are each 2.5 point ———-
  • 3rd additional command is a dq for the round

Praise

May be praised after each exercise, must be back in position within 5 seconds

item.title: Bark training 1
item.description: The goal is to give power to her barks. I want her to think she can make me come to her, or make me flee based on her barks. If I ask her to get scary, we want to see powerful barks. To get this we are going to use shaping and frustration with the tug. As she barks I’ll move closer. Initially, I should reward any vocal barks with forward motion and should avoid resetting. As time goes on I want to start getting stronger and more sustained barks. Eventually, this should turn into a game where I can move back or go still, and she’ll bring me to life with barking.

item.title: Bark training 2
item.description:
Alright this is like a week after Bark Training 1. We’ve done the tie back a few times and I think I can get her to do it with me holding her back. This can be confusing for her, and I wouldn’t want to try it if I didn’t think she was ready. And we’ll want to do this a few times before we move to having a helper.

The technique here is shaping again, this time instead of me getting closer to her, we get closer to the ball. I see some guys on youtube using agitation collars for this; but, I want to keep my leash pressure communication strong, so I’m going to use harnesses most of the time.

item.title: Detection 1
item.description: In this video I’m still trying to figure out a style of video I want to make. Look how tiny she is!!! This was something I used to do with my old dog. The scent that we decide on here doesn’t matter. If you have something specific to work on like narcotics then by all means, train with that. I’ve found that it’s pretty easy to introduce new smells later. My last dog could be turned onto a new scent with 2-3 reps of making it super easy. For IGP we are going to need her to use her nose in a different way; however, this is an easy way to work the nose every day.

item.title: Small challegnes
item.description: Also called a confidence course or maybe a DIY beginner agility course. But we spent a full deployment watching Stonnie Dennis videos so… We’re gonna call it small challenges Course. This is day 2 after building, I got my wife to come out and help establish a proper baseline for both pups.
